rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the text: it targets renters in buildings “transitioning to efficient and all[e]-electric systems,” which is a standard building-decarbonization (mitigation) pathway, and it sits under the parent statement “Eliminate fossil fuel use in existing buildings.” The gate failure (‘no Mitigation outcome token’) reflects that the action is enabling (tenant protection/anti-displacement) rather than stating emissions reductions, but it is still integral to delivering the mitigation transition in buildings. Therefore it should be retained as Mitigation rather than dropped or reclassified.
policy_statement: By 2023, develop and adopt tenant protection and anti-displacement policies for renters in buildings transitioning to efficient all-electric systems (BO.2-8).
mechanism_description: Developing and adopting tenant protection and anti-displacement policies helps renters remain in place during transitions to efficient all-electric building systems, supporting equitable implementation of electrification upgrades.
primary_causal_pathway: Tenant protections (enabling policy) → ↑ feasibility/uptake of building electrification → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: landlords/building owners can undertake electrification retrofits while tenants are protected from displacement, excessive rent increases, or harmful construction impacts, increasing acceptance and continuity of occupancy during upgrades.  
(2) Mediating physical process: electrification replaces combustion-based space/water heating and cooking with electric end-uses (often paired with efficiency), shifting energy supply away from direct fossil combustion and lowering combustion-related CO₂/CH₄ emissions (and potentially enabling cleaner grid supply over time).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: reduced building-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂ from gas/oil combustion; also reduced upstream methane leakage where relevant).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling widespread building electrification that reduces on-site fossil fuel use and associated GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: the policy’s climate impact occurs mainly by enabling electrification retrofits that cut fossil fuel combustion in buildings, reducing GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: although the policy is equity-focused, its principal climate-relevant consequence is mitigation (emissions reduction) rather than adaptation (hazard exposure reduction), resource efficiency (same service with less energy input as the main endpoint), or nature-based solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if tenant protections exist without any linkage to electrification/retrofits, the climate impact could be indirect or negligible; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly ties protections to “buildings transitioning to efficient all-electric systems,” anchoring the dominant mitigation pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy under this mechanism is designed to prevent displacement specifically to support/enable equitable electrification implementation; thus the shared dominant climate effect remains enabling electrification-driven emissions reductions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: the exact mitigation subtype (e.g., direct electrification vs. enabling governance/equity measure) and magnitude depend on policy design and accompanying electrification programs, and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented without actual electrification mandates/incentives or if protections significantly delay retrofit timelines, near-term emissions benefits could be smaller; nevertheless, the mechanism’s intended and typical dominant pathway is enabling emissions-reducing electrification rather than delivering adaptation or resource-efficiency outcomes on its own.
key_indicators: “develop and adopt tenant protection and anti-displacement policies”; “renters in buildings transitioning to efficient all-electric systems”; “By 2023”
co_benefits: Reduced displacement and housing instability; improved tenant health/safety during construction; affordability/equity protections
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be “regulatory-mandate” if the adopted policies are formal ordinances with enforceable requirements (e.g., just-cause eviction, rent caps tied to retrofits). The text, however, describes the action at the policy-development/adoption level without specifying enforceable rules or funding, so “planning-strategy” is selected.
